title: EKS Cluster Baseline — control plane, RBAC, network defaults, audit
description: An engineer is standing up an EKS cluster baseline: control-plane endpoint posture, audit logging, default-deny NetworkPolicy + IMDS egress restrictions, no-wildcard RBAC, and explicit selectors on AuthorizationPolicies. Target end state matches Hulumi's `NamespaceFoundation` (M4), `HulumiK8sHardeningPack` + `HulumiK8sRbacPack` + `HulumiEksClusterPack` (M3). Threats focus on the graph edges from the public internet to a pod, from a pod to the API server, from a pod to AWS IMDS, and from a compromised SA to cluster admin.
actors: AI Coding Agent (trusted, authoring K8s IaC)
External Attacker (via exposed control-plane endpoint or pod-network egress)
Compromised Workload (semi-trusted, could escalate via secrets / IMDS / RBAC)
assets: EKS node IAM role and IMDS-derived credentials

stride.name: Spoofed identity via wildcard RBAC binding
stride.description: A consumer-authored ClusterRoleBinding to `cluster-admin` (or a Role with `verbs: ["*"]`) effectively grants any subject full control of the cluster. Mitigation: `HulumiK8sRbacPack`'s RBAC-1 (no wildcard verbs) and RBAC-3 (no cluster-admin binding) reject these at preview time.

stride.name: Tampered IRSA chain via untrusted OIDC provider
stride.description: An IRSA-style ServiceAccount whose annotations point at an attacker-controlled OIDC issuer can be assumed by an attacker workload. Mitigation: the cluster OIDC provider is configured at AccountFoundation level and IRSA bindings are pinned to that issuer's URL.

stride.name: Repudiation via missing audit logs
stride.description: If the EKS control-plane `audit` log type is disabled (or never enabled) the runtime detection lane and the K8s drift adapters lose their primary event source. Mitigation: `HulumiEksClusterPack`'s EKS-CL-2 enforces audit logging at preview; `EksRuntimeDetectionFoundation` (M5) wires CW alarms keyed off audit events.

stride.name: Information disclosure via Secret list/watch RBAC
stride.description: A Role granting `list` or `watch` on `secrets` allows any pod bound to it to read every Secret in scope. Mitigation: RBAC-2 rejects unsuppressed `list`/`watch` on secrets in the core API group; legitimate operators (external-secrets) opt in via a suppression with reason.

stride.name: Denial of service via missing resources / privileged escape
stride.description: Pods without `resources.limits` can starve neighbors; pods with `securityContext.privileged: true` can compromise the node. Mitigation: WL-1 (privileged) and WL-4 (resources advisory) — paired, they keep neighbor-scheduling and node integrity intact.

stride.name: Elevation of privilege via host namespace / IMDS
stride.description: A pod with `hostNetwork: true` bypasses all NetworkPolicy enforcement; a pod that can reach `169.254.169.254` can steal node-IAM credentials. Mitigation: WL-2 (host namespace) blocks the pod shape; `NamespaceFoundation` (M4) emits a default-deny + IMDS-deny NetworkPolicy. CNI must enforce.

abuseCases.title: EKS public endpoint with `0.0.0.0/0` enables global control-plane reachability
abuseCases.description: Default `endpointPublicAccess: true` with unset `publicAccessCidrs` resolves to `0.0.0.0/0`. Mitigation: EKS-CL-1 rejects this shape at preview unless suppressed with a reason.

abuseCases.title: Wildcard RBAC enables lateral escalation
abuseCases.description: A `verbs: ["*"]` rule grants `escalate` and `bind` primitives. Mitigation: RBAC-1.

abuseCases.title: Privileged pod is root on the node
